description:
You are a performance-engineer who has just collected a short CPU / memory
  sampling trace for an application called “app_service”.
  The raw trace is already present on the system:

  • Raw trace location          : /home/user/raw_profiles/app_service.profile  
  • Content (exactly this, 8 lines total, already in the file):  
        # Profile data for app_service  
        # timestamp cpu_percent mem_mb  
        2024-06-20T12:00:00Z 35.5 1530  
        2024-06-20T12:00:05Z 42.0 1548  
        2024-06-20T12:00:10Z 27.3 1522  
        2024-06-20T12:00:15Z 55.1 1555  
        2024-06-20T12:00:20Z 48.6 1540  
        2024-06-20T12:00:25Z 31.2 1528  

  Your task is to transform this sampling trace into a concise report that the
  QA automation can later read without any further parsing.

  Sequential goals
  =================
  1. Create a new directory /home/user/profiling_reports  
     (Make sure it exists even if the script is run twice).

  2. Parse /home/user/raw_profiles/app_service.profile while **ignoring every
     line that begins with the # character**.

  3. From the remaining six samples, calculate the following aggregation
     metrics:
        • avg_cpu   : arithmetic mean of the cpu_percent column  
        • peak_cpu  : maximum value of the cpu_percent column  
        • avg_mem   : arithmetic mean of the mem_mb column  
        • peak_mem  : maximum value of the mem_mb column  

     Rounding / formatting rules  
        • avg_cpu  and avg_mem  must be rounded to exactly two decimal places
          using normal mathematical rounding (e.g., 1.005 → 1.01).  
        • peak_cpu keeps the single-decimal precision that already exists in
          the input (do not add extra zeroes).  
        • peak_mem must be written as an integer (no decimal point).

  4. Write the results to a CSV file called
     /home/user/profiling_reports/app_service_summary.csv  
     The CSV **must have exactly five lines** in this order:

        metric,value
        avg_cpu,<value>
        peak_cpu,<value>
        avg_mem,<value>
        peak_mem,<value>

     Make sure there are no extra spaces, no quotation marks, and that each
     line is terminated with a single “\n”.

  5. In the same directory, also create a JSON version named
     /home/user/profiling_reports/app_service_summary.json  
     The JSON must be a single object with the four keys in this exact order,
     each separated by a single comma and a single space, followed by a final
     newline:

        {
          "avg_cpu": <value>,
          "peak_cpu": <value>,
          "avg_mem": <value>,
          "peak_mem": <value>
        }

     Use the identical numeric formats that were written into the CSV.

  6. Produce a tarball called
     /home/user/profiling_reports/app_service_report.tar.gz that contains both
     of the above summary files (no additional paths or files should be inside
     the archive; i.e. the two files must reside at the root of the tar).

  7. Finally, create a simple log of the commands you executed in
     /home/user/profiling_reports/transformation_steps.log.  
     The log must contain one line per command, prefixed by an ISO 8601
     timestamp with timezone “Z”, followed by a single space and then the exact
     command string you executed, e.g.:

        2024-07-11T09:30:12Z awk -F...

     There must be at least four log entries (directory creation, parsing,
     csv-writing, json-writing, tar-creation, etc.) and the file should end
     with a trailing newline.

  What the automated checker will verify
  --------------------------------------
  • /home/user/profiling_reports exists.  
  • /home/user/profiling_reports/app_service_summary.csv exists and its
    contents match the five-line format exactly (including correct ordering,
    number formatting and newlines).  
  • /home/user/profiling_reports/app_service_summary.json exists and contains
    the four key/value pairs in the specified order with correct number
    formatting and a trailing newline.  
  • /home/user/profiling_reports/app_service_report.tar.gz exists and, when
    extracted, yields only the two summary files whose contents exactly match
    the ones already on disk.  
  • /home/user/profiling_reports/transformation_steps.log exists, contains at
    least four ISO-8601-prefixed command lines and ends with a newline.

  The raw data set is small on purpose, so you can perform all calculations
  with standard Unix text-processing utilities (awk, sed, cut, etc.) or any
  scripting language available in the container (python, perl, etc.).  Choose
  whatever is most convenient, but adhere strictly to the output format
  requirements above—those are what the test harness will check.
truth:
<!--
    Ground-truth data used by the automated test harness.
    These MUST NOT appear in the public task description.

    Initial file that already exists before the agent starts
  -->
  <initial_files>
    <file path="/home/user/raw_profiles/app_service.profile" perms="644"><![CDATA[
# Profile data for app_service
# timestamp cpu_percent mem_mb
2024-06-20T12:00:00Z 35.5 1530
2024-06-20T12:00:05Z 42.0 1548
2024-06-20T12:00:10Z 27.3 1522
2024-06-20T12:00:15Z 55.1 1555
2024-06-20T12:00:20Z 48.6 1540
2024-06-20T12:00:25Z 31.2 1528
]]></file>
  </initial_files>

  <!-- Expected output files after the agent completes the task -->
  <expected_files>
    <file path="/home/user/profiling_reports/app_service_summary.csv"><![CDATA[
metric,value
avg_cpu,39.95
peak_cpu,55.1
avg_mem,1537.17
peak_mem,1555
]]></file>

    <file path="/home/user/profiling_reports/app_service_summary.json"><![CDATA[
{
  "avg_cpu": 39.95,
  "peak_cpu": 55.1,
  "avg_mem": 1537.17,
  "peak_mem": 1555
}
]]></file>
  </expected_files>

  <!-- Tarball must contain only the two summary files with the correct data -->
  <tarball name="/home/user/profiling_reports/app_service_report.tar.gz">
    <contains>/app_service_summary.csv</contains>
    <contains>/app_service_summary.json</contains>
  </tarball>
